headline: Amazon Coffee Farmers Thrive by Protecting Forest
meta_description: Ecuadorian coffee farmers are earning premium prices by adopting forest-friendly practices, ahead of European deforestation regulations.
summary_short: Coffee farmers in Ecuador's Amazon region are earning higher prices by refusing to cut down trees, proving conservation and profit can align.
body:
In Ecuador's Zamora Chinchipe province, small-scale coffee and cacao farmers are finding that environmental stewardship pays dividends. Victoria Alverca Peña, a farmer of 25 years and co-founder of APECAP—a cooperative of small producers—speaks of the pride in protecting the forest while growing their crops. The strategy is working: farmers can now command premium prices for coffee produced without clearing trees.

This approach offers a compelling model as Europe begins enforcing stricter deforestation rules. By staying ahead of regulations, these farmers strengthen their market position while protecting the ecosystems their livelihoods depend on. The message is simple but powerful: "I take care of the environment, I don't cut down trees, and my coffee will be valued more highly."

The success of APECAP and similar initiatives shows that conservation and economic viability need not be in conflict. When farmers see direct rewards for protecting forests, both their communities and the natural world benefit.
